content.text: Every React update moves through three discrete phases:
(React calls your components to produce a new element tree), and
(React applies the minimal set of DOM changes). Understanding this pipeline explains why renders must be pure, when effects run, and what React's concurrent model can and cannot interrupt.

attrs.alt: React render pipeline: Trigger → Render → Commit → Paint & Effects
attrs.title: The four stages of a React update with their key properties and timing

content.text: A render is triggered in one of two ways: the initial mount, or a state update during an already-mounted tree.
, React schedules the first render of the entire tree. Every component in the tree runs for the first time, and React creates the DOM from scratch.
After the initial mount, any of the following causes a re-render of the relevant component and its subtree:
setter called with a new value
dispatch that returns a new state
A parent re-renders with new props for this component
value changes in a parent Provider
React 18 automatically batches all state updates — whether they occur in event handlers,
, or Promise callbacks — into a single render. This is why calling two setters in one event handler triggers only one re-render.

"Rendering" in React means calling your component functions. React walks the tree from the root (or from the component where state changed), calling each component and collecting the returned JSX. The result is a description of what the UI
look like — not the actual DOM.
The render phase must be pure
Because the render phase may be interrupted and restarted (in concurrent mode), it must not produce observable side effects. That means no DOM mutations, no network requests, no writing to a
, no subscriptions. Same input props and state must always produce the same output JSX.

After calling the components, React compares the new element tree with the previous one (reconciliation). It builds a list of the minimal DOM operations needed — which nodes to insert, update, or remove. This comparison runs in O(n) time because React uses two heuristics:
produce a completely new tree (the old one is destroyed).
Elements with the same type at the same position are
by comparing their props. The DOM node is reused.
Keys tell React which tree position corresponds to which list item across renders, enabling it to match children by identity rather than position.

calls each component function twice and discards the second result. This is intentional: if your component is pure (same input → same output), calling it twice produces identical JSX. Any impure side effect becomes visible as a double execution.
If you see a console.log print twice in development, your component is pure. If the two prints show different values or cause different side effects, it is impure — fix the component, not the log.
Once reconciliation is complete, React enters the commit phase. This is when the actual DOM is modified. Unlike the render phase, the commit phase is
— React never interrupts a commit once it starts. This guarantees the browser never sees a half-updated DOM.
The commit phase has three sub-steps:
— React applies DOM insertions, updates, and deletions
to the DOM node during the layout sub-step — after the DOM mutation but before
during the render phase and populated for the rest of the commit and effect phases.
After Commit — Paint and Passive Effects
After the commit phase, the browser paints the updated pixels. Only after paint does React fire
). This ordering ensures that effects do not block the user from seeing the new UI.
effect's cleanup before running the new setup. The sequence is:

React 18 introduced concurrent rendering, where the render phase can be interrupted, paused, or abandoned. React may start rendering a component tree and then discard the work entirely if a higher-priority update arrives — for example, a user typing while a slow list is rendering.
The commit phase is never interrupted, which guarantees the DOM stays consistent. Only the render phase is subject to interruption, which is why render must be pure.
render if the user types again, re-starting it with the newer query. Without this, React would complete the old render first and then start a new one.
Fires on every render, doubles in StrictMode
null during render; node not yet committed
Runs after paint — visible flicker if layout adjusts
Both hooks run after the commit phase, but at different times relative to the browser paint. The rule is straightforward:
for everything that does not require reading or writing the DOM synchronously — data fetching, event subscriptions, logging, third-party library setup.
only when you must measure or mutate the DOM before the browser paints — tooltip positioning, scroll restoration, focus management.
blocks paint and can hurt perceived performance if it does expensive work.
React can short-circuit the render phase entirely when it detects that a component's output cannot have changed. Three mechanisms trigger bailouts:
— wraps a component; re-renders only if props shallowly changed
setter (Object.is comparison) — React skips the re-render
— memoize derived values and functions to avoid breaking

React DevTools Profiler records each render cycle. Each bar represents one commit. The height represents commit duration; the color represents performance (green = fast, yellow/red = slow). Click any bar to see which components rendered and why ("Props changed", "State changed", "Hooks changed", "Parent rendered").
A component that shows "Parent rendered" in the Profiler may be a candidate for
if it renders often and is expensive. Components that show "Props changed" need stable references from
The render() Function Is Called Recursively
When React renders a component, it may encounter child components in the returned JSX. React then renders those children too, recursively, until it reaches host elements (
. This traversal is depth-first: React fully renders a branch before moving to the next sibling.

React does not commit any DOM change until the entire tree's render phase is complete. If an error is thrown in any component during this traversal, React catches it via the nearest error boundary and commits an error UI instead.
What Happens on First Render vs Re-Render
The first render and subsequent re-renders share the same three-phase pipeline but differ in what the commit phase does:
Cleanup of old, then new setup (if deps changed)
Yes, for matching component type at same position
Triggering a Render Inside an Effect

is legal and common (e.g., after a data fetch). Doing so triggers an additional render cycle: React schedules a new render, runs it, commits the updated DOM, then runs effects again if any dependencies changed.
from running after the component unmounts or
changes — a common source of memory-leak warnings. Always return a cleanup function when an effect starts async work.
Three built-in mechanisms prevent endless render cycles:
— if you call a setter with the identical value (
when count is already 5), React skips the render entirely.
only re-runs when listed dependencies actually change. An empty
means "run once on mount, never on updates".
— in concurrent mode, if a low-priority update keeps re-triggering, React's scheduler eventually deprioritizes it, preventing a CPU lockup.
A React update has three phases: Trigger (something requests a render), Render (component functions produce an element tree), and Commit (React applies DOM changes).
The render phase must be pure — no side effects. It can be interrupted in concurrent mode.
The commit phase is always synchronous — React never interrupts a DOM update.
runs synchronously after DOM mutation, before paint. Use it only for DOM measurements.
(passive effects) runs asynchronously after paint. Use it for data fetching, subscriptions, and other non-visual work.
Refs are populated during the layout sub-step of commit —
React 18 batches all state updates automatically.
marks a render as low-priority and interruptible.
